topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a jarring intrusion into an otherwise quiet afternoon. A profile picture loaded: a grainy, overly-filtered selfie of Mark, the architect of most of my awkward middle school years. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I stared at the request, a slow, deliberate drag of my thumb across the screen. *Seriously?* I thought, a sharp, brittle feeling settling in my chest. I immediately swiped it away, the gesture swift and dismissive, hoping the digital ghost would vanish. It didn't. A second message followed, simple and direct: "Hey. Long time no talk." I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning under my weight. I picked up my lukewarm coffee, the ceramic feeling suddenly too hot in my hand. I took a deep, slow breath, trying to smooth down the sudden tension radiating through my shoulders. I definitely wasn't interested in reopening any old wounds.

[story 2]

I glanced at my phone as if it were a live grenade. Another request. Mark. The name alone sent a low thrum of annoyance through me. My fingers hovered over the 'view profile' button, then retreated. I could practically feel the heat rising in my neck. It was such a predictable move, this attempt to resurface the past. I let out a small, involuntary sigh, a sound laced with pure exasperation. I scrolled through my recent messages, finding nothing remotely familiar or appealing. I deliberately minimized the notification, focusing instead on the intricate patterns on my desk. When the second message arrived—a casual 'What's up?'—I didn't even look up. I simply tapped the 'ignore' button with unnecessary force. The faint tremor in my hand betrayed how much I wanted to slam the device face down.

[story 3]

When the alert flashed, a distinct clenching sensation seized my stomach. It was that familiar, unwelcome tightening that always accompanied contact from Mark. I set my laptop aside with a sharp click, the sound echoing in the suddenly too-still room. I felt a distinct urge to pace, to physically move away from the screen, but I stayed rooted. I read the request, a slow, deliberate scan of the familiar username. A low, almost inaudible scoff escaped my lips. Why now? What was he expecting? I typed a brief, curt reply, ensuring the tone was ice-cold, every word calibrated to convey utter disinterest. I watched the 'sent' confirmation, a small, self-satisfied smirk touching the corner of my mouth. The whole interaction felt like a tiresome chore I had to complete before I could return to something more engaging.
